course_description:
This course teaches students to critically pair creative content and information with digital media. It also introduces students to both analyse and create transmedia narratives, drawing on a variety of different genres and media platforms. In producing their own transmedia stories, students will hone a set of media production skills, including audio-visual digital media as well as verbal presentation skills.

This course introduces students to the principles and practice of transmedia storytelling : the design of narratives that unfold across multiple media platforms, each contributing uniquely to a wider storyworld. As the media landscape continues to evolve, the ability to craft stories that move between formats is an essential skill for contemporary communicators, journalists and creative practitioners.

Students will explore how stories can shift, stretch and transform as they move through different platforms. Through a combination of workshops, hands‑on production, and analysis of current industry examples, students will learn how different medium’s shape communication, audience engagement, and narrative meaning.

Working in creative teams, students will use industry‑standard tools to develop an original transmedia storyworld with a clear purpose and strategic intent. The course emphasises practical experimentation, creative problem‑solving, and reflective practice.

By the end of the semester, students will have produced a portfolio‑ready transmedia project that showcases their ability to communicate ideas clearly and creatively across multiple platforms. The skills developed in this course are transferable to a wide range of professional contexts including journalism, strategic communication, digital media production, and any field where compelling storytelling is essential.

incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Whilst every effort is made to place students in their preferred activity, it is not always possible for a student to be enrolled in their tutorial of choice. If you require assistance, please ensure that you email
timetabling.commarts@enquire.uq.edu.au
from your
UQ student email
with:
Your name
Your student ID
The course code
A list of three tutorial preferences (in order of preference)
Reason for the change – e.g. timetable clash, elite athlete status, SAP
Teaching staff do not have access to change tutorials or help with timetables; all timetabling changes must be processed through the Timetabling Team.
ACTIVITIES ASSOCIATED WITH THIS COURSE
1 Tutorials:
Tutorials are where you discuss ideas with your colleagues and tutors and get feedback on your work. Please ensure that you successfully sign up for a tutorial (T Group) in Allocate by the first week of class and show up to that same tutorial and that tutorial only throughout the semester. Tutorials commence in Week 2. If you show up to a tutorial you are not enrolled in, you will be asked to leave. This is because the main assessment for this course is a group project and we need to keep the same people in their assigned tutorial over the semester.
The tutorials are organised around smaller cohorts (20-25 students) and will involve more hands-on group exercises and learning that incorporates ongoing tutor feedback. If you do not attend any tutorials by Tutorial 4, you will be in danger of failing the course entirely and may be advised to withdraw.
Each week, you are asked to complete a module on Blackboard and come to the tutorial prepared. The modules introduce key concepts in narrative theory, transmedia theory, and how to work across multiple media formats. They also give you important case studies of existing transmedia stories that will help you generate ideas for your own work.
Missing tutorials:
When you skip class, it leaves your team members in a difficult situation, and you miss important information and discussions designed to help you do well in the assessments. There is a strong correlation between students who do well in the assessments and those who attend tutorials.
2 Lectures:
We have face to face lectures starting in Week 1. The lectures will also be available as video recordings on Blackboard, after their scheduled time. Please try to attend lectures in person. This is another opportunity to engage with other students and ask questions to the lecturer.
3 Production Skills Workshops:
Tutorials centre on group work and conceptual ideas introduced in the course material. The main technical nitty-gritty software skills are taught through workshops offered by an on-campus department called
Jactech
and this will be discussed in further detail at the first lecture.
4 Be on top of the Course Activities
Failing to engage with the online components of the course or the technical skills learning resources available to you will reflect in poor performance on your assignments.
Missing tutorials regularly will result in poor performance in the assignments as well as negative peer assessment from your team members.
course_aims: The course teaches you to spread a transmedia story-world across multiple media platforms with purpose and criticality, matching media content to the media platform that suits it best. This class involves working in groups to develop and ultimately pitch your cross platform story-world.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate understanding of how creative content behaves in different media contexts and adapts to different understandings of user engagement.
Work effectively in creative teams to pitch a multimedia storyworld in the form of a formal presentation involving all team members.
Produce original media assets that showcase a transmedia storyworld, drawing on practices such as photography, graphic design, illustration, and audio-visual production.
Critically explain and justify creative decisions in a media portfolio through application of transmedia concepts and relevant theoretical ideas covered in the course.

assessment_details.title: Portfolio Consultations
assessment_details.task_description: Students should attend two individual in-class consultations with their tutor scheduled during the semester. Assessment 1 part A is scheduled before the midterm break, and Assessment 1 part B is scheduled in the second half of the course. These consultations are centred around the students’ portfolio work (submitted to Blackboard before the consultations). Students will explain and justify their creative and technical decisions, demonstrating critical analysis skills developed in the course, by using concepts and vocabulary pertinent to transmedia storytelling. The Assessment 1 has 2 components: Portfolio entry: Submission of portfolio process to Blackboard BEFORE class. This can be notes, sketches, drawings, mindmaps, storyboards or any other format that shows your thinking process towards working to the final portfolio. Assessment 1 Part B must show improvement/progress from what was submitted to Assessment 1 part A. Consultations: In-class consultations with the tutor, where students explain their portfolio work. No notes or machine translation tools can be used during the consultation. Full details and a comprehensive grading rubric are provided on Blackboard under "Assessments". AI Policy This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Students should submit their portfolio entry on Blackboard before their scheduled consultations. If the student comes to class for the consultation but has nothing uploaded on Blackboard, they won’t be allowed to sit in the consultation. Students with a medical certificate for the date of their scheduled assessment should inform their tutor before the consultation to be allowed to attend a catch-up consultation with the course coordinator at another date. Assessment 1 part B must show some improvement/progress from what was submitted to Assessment 1 part A.
Blackboard Assignments
This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard Assignments. Before submitting any assignments for this course, you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
Important submission information
You must click on the Submit button to submit your work for assessment. Academic staff will not see the files if you have merely saved them as a submission in progress. Files that are submitted CANNOT be retrieved for editing and re-submission. Once submitted, they are delivered to the Course Coordinator. It is your responsibility to check the Submission History and confirm your assignment was successfully submitted. To allow for the many possible technical problems with computers and the internet, students are advised to commence assignment uploads at least 3 hours before they are due. If you don’t receive a submission ID, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. It is your responsibility to check the assignment preview and confirm that the assignment has been successfully submitted. Students should take a screenshot showing the successful submission to confirm that they have followed the correct process.
Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your Blackboard assignment .
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Students are offered the option to choose between two set dates for each of the consultations. Students with a medical certificate for the date of their scheduled assessment should inform their tutor before the consultation to be allowed to attend a catch-up consultation with the course coordinator at another date.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .

assessment_details.title: Midterm Project
assessment_details.due_date: 17/04/2026 4:00 pm Portfolio and Statement: Due date listed above.
assessment_details.task_description: You will work in a creative team (3 to 5 students) to create a transmedia storyworld that exists authentically across multiple platforms. Each team member is responsible for one platform/medium, ensuring that the story elements respond and capitalise on each medium’s unique features (medium specificity). Assessment components (both to be submitted individually on Blackboard): Media portfolio Statement explaining your submission 1 Portfolio: Each person in the team must produce a media portfolio of creative and original work using the Adobe Suite flagship programs (Photoshop, Premiere Pro, Audition, Illustrator, InDesign). We want you to use these software because they are the industry standard. At this point, we are interested in your plans, no polished work is expected for this assessment. You can use a variety of different formats, such as drawing and illustration, still photography, posters, audio-visual material, including video or music, among others. We are open to your ideas! Examples of adequately substantial individual media portfolio submitted at the mid-semester stage will be shown and discussed in tutorials and lectures. Original content only: No stock images or footage (except scoring/music). Only content produced during the period of this course (no old pictures/footage). Process Evidence Requirement: You must provide clear evidence of your creative and technical process, such as: Screenshots of Adobe timelines/layers Drafts, early exports, failed tests Original assets files Version history Prompt logs (if AI/MT was used). This evidence supports secure assessment and authorship verification. We want to see that you have used the Adobe Creative suite with minimal to no use of AI tools. 2 Statement: With your media portfolio, you must submit a written statement explaining your submission. Students must answer the following questions: What is your team storyworld about? How is your media output contributing to your team storyworld? How did you create this work? How was the Adobe Suite used? How are you capitalising on medium specificity? Demonstrate how the course’s ideas have informed your work. Did you use AI or Machine Translation tools at any stage? If yes, specify which tools, how, and why, and what parts of the work are fully your own and what parts are AI generated. Was it helpful? How? Justify the use of these tools. Full details and a comprehensive grading rubric are provided on Blackboard under "Assessments". AI Policy This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

Portfolio and Statement: Submitted via Blackboard. Large files should be submitted as links to online folders (like OneDrive) or online platforms (like YouTube). Each person is responsible for submitting their own materials that they want marked on Blackboard.

It is your responsibility to check the assignment preview and confirm that the assignment has been successfully submitted. Students should take a screenshot showing the successful submission to confirm that they have followed the correct process.
Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your Blackboard assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Final Project
assessment_details.due_date: 29/05/2026 4:00 pm Pitch: In Week 13 tutorials. Portfolio and Statements: Due date listed above.
assessment_details.task_description: For this task, students will continue working in the same creative teams. Assessment 3 has four components, each marked individually: Pitch/Presentation: Presented in class to your peers (2-3 minutes for each person in the group). Media portfolio (submitted individually on Blackboard). Statement explaining your submission (submitted to Blackboard with the media portfolio). Peer Assessment Statement addressing the group dynamics (submitted to Blackboard with the media portfolio). 1 Pitch/Presentation: Students work in the same creative teams assigned for Assessment 2. The team will pitch a transmedia storyworld (either fictional or nonfictional) that exists across as many media platforms as there are people in that group. At this stage, your project must justify how it wants to treat the user/audience and create pathways for audience participation in your storyworld. The pitch should mirror professional industry standards and must introduce what your storyworld is about, what your media platform is contributing to the overall idea, what kind of audience you have in mind, what opportunities the audience has for participation, and lastly, what you made and why. Each person in the team must present what they created and explain how it connects with the overall team idea. All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device) Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course. Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of: Moderation of marking; Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or Re-marking following a successful re-mark application. 2 Portfolio: At this stage, the individual portfolio should demonstrate noticeable improvement from the first submission (Assessment 2), including new media developed since the midterm, pushing your storyworld concept further. You are expected to work with the Adobe Suite flagship programs (Photoshop, Premiere Pro, Audition, Illustrator, InDesign) in the production of your portfolio. Examples of adequately substantial individual final media portfolios will be shown and discussed in tutorials and lectures. Original content only: No stock images or footage (except scoring/music). Only content produced during the period of this course (no old pictures/footage). Process Evidence Requirement: You must provide clear evidence of your creative and technical process, such as: Screenshots of Adobe timelines/layers Drafts, early exports, failed tests Original assets files Version history Prompt logs (if AI/MT was used). This evidence supports secure assessment and authorship verification. We want to see that you have used the Adobe Creative suite with minimal to no use of AI tools. 3 Statement: A statement that describes the rationale behind the final submission and how it connects to the larger project. The statement should capture the student’s approach to their component of the final project and detail how the project connects to key ideas discussed during this course. Your statement should: Describe how the Adobe Suite was used in this project, highlighting key issues/opportunities encountered in using these programs. Justify your creative choices, using the key concepts discussed in this course. Which concepts were most relevant in your work? Explain the “why” behind your creative choices. Explain how you considered and fostered audience participation. Did you use AI or Machine Translation tools at any stage? If yes, specify which tools, how, and why, and what parts of the work are fully your own and what parts are AI generated. Was it helpful? How? Justify the use of these tools. 4 Peer Assessment Statement : A statement addressing the group dynamics: How was the work divided within your team, and did this approach work well? Were there any issues with collaboration, and if so, how were they managed? What were your individual responsibilities, and how did you contribute? Did you need to complete any tasks assigned to others? What challenges did you face, and what might you do differently next time? Full details and a comprehensive grading rubric are provided on Blackboard under "Assessments". AI Policy This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Pitch/Presentation: Will take place during the Week 13 tutorial. Missing the group pitch without communicating with tutors beforehand and without an appropriate reason will result in an automatic fail for your group component. Please note that vacations or other work commitments are not acceptable excuses for missing this presentation date.
Portfolio, Statement and Peer Assessment Statement: Submitted via Blackboard. Large files should be submitted as links to online folders (like OneDrive) or online platforms (like YouTube). Each person is responsible for submitting their own materials that they want marked on Blackboard.
Blackboard Assignments
This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard Assignments. Before submitting any assignments for this course, you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
Important submission information
You must click on the Submit button to submit your work for assessment. Academic staff will not see the files if you have merely saved them as a submission in progress. Files that are submitted CANNOT be retrieved for editing and re-submission. Once submitted, they are delivered to the Course Coordinator. It is your responsibility to check the Submission History and confirm your assignment was successfully submitted. To allow for the many possible technical problems with computers and the internet, students are advised to commence assignment uploads at least 3 hours before they are due. If you don’t receive a submission ID, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. It is your responsibility to check the assignment preview and confirm that the assignment has been successfully submitted. Students should take a screenshot showing the successful submission to confirm that they have followed the correct process.
Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your Blackboard assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
The pitch/presentation component is to be completed in person. Students are NOT able to apply for an extension via the Unitask portal for the pitch/presentation. Please contact your tutor/course coordinator directly to see if alternative arrangements are available.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
